name: Parker Mountain
short_description: BLM public rockhounding on Parker Mountain near Lemhi produces quartz, chalcedony, and agate specimens. No permit required - ideal for beginner collectors seeking Central Idaho gemstone material.
description:
Parker Mountain sits within Lemhi County's productive rockhounding region, where BLM-managed public land welcomes recreational mineral collectors. This site offers accessible collecting for quartz-based minerals including chalcedony, agate, and jasper specimens typical of Idaho's central mountain zone. The landscape combines open hillsides with scattered outcrops where weathering naturally exposes gem-quality material. Collectors typically work the loose talus slopes and wash areas at the base of exposures, where gravity and seasonal water movement concentrate lighter specimens. This location suits both beginners learning to identify mineral forms and experienced rockhounds seeking quality Idaho quartz varieties.

The geology here reflects the Salmon River Mountains' Precambrian and Paleozoic basement rocks interspersed with Tertiary silica-rich deposits. Hydrothermal activity during mountain building created veins and replacement bodies rich in microcrystalline quartz and chalcedony, which weather out of hillsides as individual specimens. Parker Mountain's position along this mineralized corridor makes it a reliable spot for consistent collecting without the travel time required for more remote central Idaho sites.

Expect moderate elevation terrain with typical high-desert mountain vegetation including sagebrush and scattered conifers. Access is straightforward via BLM roads, and the site accommodates day trips from Lemhi or longer exploring the surrounding public lands. Bring water, wear sturdy boots for rocky terrain, and plan to spend 3-4 hours working productive zones.
history: Parker Mountain lies within the Salmon River Mountains, a region shaped by Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks and Paleozoic marine sediments. Tertiary hydrothermal activity - associated with the Eocene-Oligocene volcanism and crustal extension that characterized central Idaho - drove silica-rich fluids through existing fault and fracture systems, creating the quartz and chalcedony veins that weather into collectable specimens today. Early 20th-century prospectors worked claims throughout this district for precious metals, but the area's gem potential for collectors was recognized later as BLM recreational rockhounding expanded.
tips: Work the talus slopes and open hillsides where quartz and chalcedony naturally concentrate - look for translucent or milky white fragments weathering from light-colored outcrops. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and hand tools (small pry bar, chisel) to carefully extract specimens from loose matrix; avoid hammering at in-place bedrock per BLM guidelines. Search near drainage washes where seasonal runoff has exposed fresh material, and focus on light-colored sandy areas where agate nodules and chalcedony chunks are most visible. Peak collecting occurs after spring snowmelt when new material is freshly exposed.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 6500-7200 feet with open sagebrush and scattered lodgepole pine forest. Hillsides slope moderately to steeply in places; walking distance from parking typically 0.5-2 miles depending on chosen collecting area. Footing is rocky with loose talus and uneven ground; sturdy boots essential. No developed trails, but terrain is navigable for fit hikers. Minimal shade on south-facing slopes.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation (6500+ ft) remains snow-covered October - May. Summer offers stable weather, dry footing, and maximum daylight for productive collecting. Early June may still have patches of snow in shaded draws; plan accordingly.
written_directions: From Lemhi, take ID 93 north approximately 8 miles. Turn east onto Forest Road 1405 (BLM maintained dirt road, suitable for high-clearance vehicles). Continue approximately 5-7 miles, watching for BLM rockhounding area signs or informal pullouts on the north side of the road where exposed quartz outcrops are visible. Park in a wide pullout and walk upslope toward visible white/gray mineral exposures. Exact turnoff coordinates should be verified with current BLM Salmon River District maps, as road numbers may change.
road_conditions: BLM dirt road, generally well-maintained but uneven with possible washboard in wet season. High-clearance vehicle recommended; standard passenger cars should proceed cautiously. Road may be rough after spring snowmelt or heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM road near rockhounding areas. No developed lot; park in wide roadside clearings where other vehicles are visible. Capacity limited to 2-3 vehicles per typical pullout. Leave clearance for other traffic.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection or resale. No motorized excavation or mechanical removal of rock. Hand tools only. Do not damage living vegetation or create new roads/disturbance. Leave the site as you found it. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sales
Hand tools only - no motorized equipment
No damage to vegetation
No new excavation or major disturbance
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Lemhi, approximately 8-12 miles south. Limited services; nearest full-service town is Salmon, about 25 miles. Bring water, fuel, and supplies before traveling to the site.
accessibility_notes: Open hillside terrain with moderate slopes and loose rocky footing. No wheelchair access. Well-conditioned hikers can manage the 0.5-2 mile walks to productive areas. Uneven ground, talus scrambling in some zones, and limited shade on south-facing slopes. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or those unable to navigate loose rock terrain.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Parker Mountain near Lemhi, Idaho?
faq.answer: Parker Mountain is known for quartz, chalcedony, agate, and jasper specimens. Most material is microcrystalline quartz in shades of white, milky translucent, and occasionally banded patterns. Collectors consistently find hand-sized to fist-sized specimens weathering from hillside exposures and talus slopes.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Parker Mountain, Idaho?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Parker Mountain sits on BLM public land where recreational rockhounding is allowed without advance permission. You may collect up to 25 pounds per day under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) using hand tools only.

faq.question: How do I find quartz and chalcedony at Parker Mountain rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Search the talus slopes and open hillsides where light-colored quartz naturally concentrates. Look for whitish or gray fragments weathering from exposed veins in the hillside. Drainage washes and sandy areas are most productive because seasonal water movement exposes fresh material. Work areas where previous collectors have searched - these spots are productive for a reason.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Parker Mountain near Lemhi?
faq.answer: June through September is optimal. The site sits at 6500+ feet elevation where snow covers the ground October through May. Summer offers stable weather, dry footing, and maximum daylight. Early June may still have snow patches in shaded areas, so plan for that possibility.

faq.question: Is Parker Mountain a beginner-friendly rockhounding location in Idaho?
faq.answer: Yes, Parker Mountain is ideal for beginners. The site requires no permit, no special skills, and specimens weather naturally from hillsides where they are relatively easy to spot and extract by hand. Easy moderate terrain is accessible to most hikers, and you can collect quality material on your first visit.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Parker Mountain, Idaho?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, chisel, and 5-gallon bucket for specimens. Wear sturdy boots because terrain is rocky and uneven at 6500+ feet elevation. Pack plenty of water (the site is remote), sunscreen, hat, and a backpack for carrying specimens up to 25 pounds. High-clearance vehicle is recommended for the BLM dirt road access.

meta_title: Parker Mountain Rockhounding - Idaho Quartz & Chalcedony
